It's important to recognize that I've arranged the order of the modules in this training program to fit with the insights of my colleague Dr. Gregory Neidert regarding how certain principles of influence are more useful than others depending on which persuasive goal the communicator wishes to achieve with a message. Of course, any persuader wants to create change in others, but according to Dr. Neidert's core motives model of social influence, the communicator's prime goal at the time affects which principles the communicator should prioritize. For instance, the model asserts that one of the main motives or goals of a persuader involves cultivating a positive relationship. Research shows that messages are more likely to be successful if recipients can first be made to feel positively toward the messenger. Three of the seven principles of influence, reciprocity, liking, and unity, are particularly appropriate to this task. Some signals that it's time to employ this set of principles would be that the other individual doesn't accept your phone calls or your invitations for an online meeting or it takes a long time for them to reply to an email if at all. Now, in other situations, perhaps when a good relationship is already in place, the goal of reducing uncertainty will be a priority. After all, having a positive relationship with a communicator doesn't necessarily mean message recipients are going to be persuaded. Before they're likely to change their minds, people want to be assured that any decision they're being urged to make is a wise one. Under these circumstances, according to the model, the principles of social proof and authority should never be ignored because evidence that a choice is well regarded by peers or by experts makes it indeed appear wise. Here, some signals that you need to reduce another's uncertainty about your proposal by using authority and social proof would be that they say they have to think about your proposal more or they have to discuss it with a partner or they keep asking questions about aspects of your offer that you've already covered. But even with a positive relationship cultivated and uncertainty reduction accomplished, a remaining goal needs to be achieved to boost the likelihood of behavioral change. We've all been there, haven't we? We know we have a good relationship with another who tells us they are sure we are the right fit for their challenge, but they don't move. In such a situation, the goal of motivating action becomes the main objective. That is, a well-liked friend may show me sufficient proof that almost everyone believes the daily exercise is a good thing and leading medical experts overwhelmingly support its health benefits, but that may not be enough to get me to do it. The friend would do well to include in any appeal the principles of consistency and scarcity, but reminding me, for example, of what I've said publicly in the past about the importance of my health, consistency, and about the unique enjoyments I would miss if I lost my health, scarcity. It's the message that would most likely move me to act. Consequently, it's the message with the best chance to get me up in the morning and off to the gym. Thus, the sequencing of the modules of this training program takes into account which principles are particularly suited to achieving these three motives of persuaders. Reciprocation, liking, and unity, for when relationship cultivation is primary. Followed by social proof and authority, for when reducing uncertainty is foremost. Out in turn, by consistency and scarcity, for when motivating action is the principal objective. It's important to recognize I am not suggesting that these three sets of principles are the sole options for achieving their respective goals. Rather, I'm only suggesting that if they are available for achieving a particular goal you have of cultivating a relationship or reducing uncertainty or motivating action, failing to target that particular set of principles for the achievement of that particular goal would be a mistake.
